clinical_history: This ECG is notable for all of the following EXCEPT?
diagnosis:
The rhythm is sinus with a prominent left bundle branch block (LBBB) pattern (QRS duration 0.15-0.16 second) with LAD. The ST-T abnormalities are consistent with the LBBB, although other causes cannot be excluded.  There are frequent premature atrial complexes (PACs) which appear in a bigeminal pattern, accounting for the group beating pattern. This paired pattern simulates sinus rhythm with AV Wenckebach with 3:2 conduction. However, in the latter case, the P waves are the same and come on time. In this case, the second P wave in each couplet is premature and has a different morphology, since it comes from an ectopic atrial site.  R wave progression is slow due to the LBBB. The biphasic P waves with a prominent negative component in lead V1 are indicative of left atrial abnormality.  
A number of findings here suggest severe underlying myocardial disease, including the LBBB with left axis deviation and the left atrial abnormality. A subtle but important supportive finding is the distinct notching of the ascending part of the S wave in lead V5 with the LBBB. This finding (in leads V3-V5 with LBBB) is referred to as Cabreras sign and is a marker of myocardial scarring due to myocardial infarction or other causes (e.g., cardiomyopathy) with this ventricular conduction disturbance.

This patient has a diagnosis of chemotherapy-induced cardiomyopathy. Biventricular heart failure was present with severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 20-25% and both mitral and tricuspid regurgitation.
